chunk_summary: Cystic fibrosis nutrition targets focus on growth-linked outcomes, energy needs exceeding healthy peers, and precise PERT titration to manage malabsorption and improve weight velocity.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of nutrition as a disease-modifying therapy in cystic fibrosis.
Learn how to set growth-linked nutritional targets for children with CF based on age-specific percentiles.
Master the principles of PERT titration, including dosing guidelines and monitoring endpoints.
Recognize the importance of early intervention with enteral feeding to prevent severe wasting.
key_facts: For children under 2 years, aim for weight-for-length (WFL) at or above the 50th percentile; for ages 2–20 years, aim for BMI at or above the 50th percentile.
Energy needs in CF often range from 110–200% of typical requirements, individualized to infection burden and growth response.
PERT dosing: 2,000–4,000 lipase units per 120 mL formula for infants; 500–2,500 units/kg/meal and 250–1,250 units/kg/snack for older children.
Fecal elastase <200 µg/g confirms exocrine pancreatic insufficiency; if unavailable, use supervised PERT trials based on symptoms and weight velocity.
Avoid sustained PERT doses >10,000 units/kg/day or >6,000 units/kg/meal to prevent complications.
reference_quote: Exocrine pancreatic insufficiency is a dominant, treatable driver of malnutrition, and PERT functions like a high-impact 'nutrition drug.'
document_key_topics: Growth-linked nutritional targets in cystic fibrosis
Early intervention with enteral feeding to prevent wasting
